title: Rolling Lumber Storage Cart
summary: Three vertical 2x4 rack frames on a rolling base — stores full-length lumber upright instead of leaned against a wall.
description:
Lumber leaned against a shop wall is lumber that falls over, warps against the floor, and blocks whatever's behind it. This cart holds full-length boards **upright and separated**, in three slotted bays, and rolls wherever you need it.

It's built entirely from 2x4s — no plywood, no sheet goods, no joinery beyond butted screws. That's deliberate: a lumber cart made from anything fancier than construction lumber is a cart you'll feel bad scratching, and this one is going to get scratched.

Size the bay spacing to your actual lumber pile before you cut anything. The dimensions here fit full sheets standing on edge in the back bay and dimensional lumber up front — adjust if your mix is different.

tools.note: Or a miter saw — mostly crosscuts on 2x4 stock.
Holds frames square while you screw them, especially working solo.

materials.name: Construction lumber, 2x4, 10 ft
materials.note: Line total for all ten boards.

materials.name: Locking swivel casters, 5"
materials.note: Line total for all four — a loaded lumber cart is heavy and low-clearance casters bind on shop floor debris.

cutList.note: Divides the bottom frame into the three storage bays.
Two per rack frame, three rack frames.
Ties the three rack frames together at the top.

steps.title: Build the bottom frame
steps.body:
Screw the two side rails, two end pieces, and four middle dividers into a rectangular frame with three evenly spaced openings. Two screws per joint, driven with the star bit screws.

Get this frame square before moving on — every rack frame above it registers off this base, so an out-of-square bottom telegraphs up into a cart that leans.

steps.title: Build the three upright rack frames
steps.body: Build three identical ladder-style frames from the upright legs and rungs, two screws per joint. These are what actually separate and support the standing lumber.

steps.title: Stand the rack frames on the base
steps.body:
Level the bottom frame first, then stand each rack frame at 90 degrees to the base, checking plumb with a square before driving four screws per joint.

Do this one frame at a time rather than all three loosely stacked — a rack frame that's slightly out of plumb is much easier to correct before the next one is screwed in next to it.

steps.title: Tie the tops together
steps.body:
Cut and install the four top tie-in pieces connecting the tops of the three rack frames, either with pocket screws from inside or angled star bit screws from top and bottom.

This is what stops the cart racking side to side once it's loaded and rolling — don't skip it even though the bottom frame already feels solid.

steps.title: Mount the casters
steps.body:
Attach the four locking casters to the underside of the bottom frame, screwing into both the frame rails and the middle dividers where possible for a wider fastening pattern.

Check that all four lock before loading the cart — a cart that rolls away while you're pulling a 10-foot board off the top rack is a hazard, not a convenience.
